era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt, mature Mycenaean civilization, Hittite Empire consolidation. International trade networks are robust; chariot warfare dominates military strategy. This is a period of relative stability before the upheavals of the 13th century BCE.

events.title: Continuation of Egyptian military campaigns in Syria-Palestine
events.description: Egyptian forces under the pharaoh (likely Amenhotep II or his predecessor) conduct campaigns to maintain control over vassal states in Syria-Palestine. These campaigns are documented in temple inscriptions and served to secure trade routes and tribute payments. The military organization reflects the sophisticated chariot corps and professional army that characterized the New Kingdom.
events.certainty_note: Specific campaigns are dated to the reign of Amenhotep II, but exact year-to-year chronology is uncertain; ±5–10 year margin.

events.title: Shang Dynasty in China during mature phase
events.description: The Shang Dynasty in China is in its mature phase, with a well-established capital and sophisticated bronze-working technology. Oracle bone inscriptions record divination practices, royal genealogies, and administrative activities. The Shang maintain extensive trade networks within East Asia and control significant territory in the Yellow River valley.
events.certainty_note: Shang chronology is debated; traditional dates place this period in the reign of a specific king, but exact synchronization with Western calendar is uncertain (±10–20 years).

disconfirming_evidence: Shang Dynasty chronology remains one of the most contested areas in Chinese historical studies. Traditional Chinese sources place the Shang much earlier than archaeological evidence supports. Modern scholarship has revised dates significantly, but disagreement persists between the 'short' and 'long' chronologies, creating uncertainty of ±10–20 years for any specific year in this period.
historiographic_note: The year 1581 BCE is poorly documented in most regions. Egyptian and Hittite sources are our primary windows into this period, but they focus on elite political and military activities. The Shang oracle bones provide unique insight into Chinese civilization but are limited in scope. Most of the world's population and regions leave no written record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same pharaonic reign and military campaign cycle
